title: MLP-Mixer: An all-MLP Architecture for Vision

paragraphs.text:
## **MLP-Mixer: An all-MLP Architecture for Vision**

**Ilya Tolstikhin** _[∗]_ **, Neil Houlsby** _[∗]_ **, Alexander Kolesnikov** _[∗]_ **, Lucas Beyer** _[∗]_ **, Xiaohua Zhai, Thomas Unterthiner, Jessica Yung, Andreas Steiner, Daniel Keysers, Jakob Uszkoreit, Mario Lucic, Alexey Dosovitskiy**

> _∗_ equal contribution Google Research, Brain Team

`{tolstikhin, neilhoulsby, akolesnikov, lbeyer, xzhai, unterthiner, jessicayung` _[†]_ `, andstein, keysers, usz, lucic, adosovitskiy}@google.com`

> _†_ work done during Google AI Residency

## **Abstract**

Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are the go-to model for computer vision. Recently, attention-based networks, such as the Vision Transformer, have also become popular. In this paper we show that while convolutions and attention are both sufficient for good performance, neither of them are necessary. We present _MLP-Mixer_ , an architecture based exclusively on multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs). MLP-Mixer contains two types of layers: one with MLPs applied independently to image patches (i.e. “mixing” the per-location features), and one with MLPs applied across patches (i.e. “mixing” spatial information). When trained on large datasets, or with modern regularization schemes, MLP-Mixer attains competitive scores on image classification benchmarks, with pre-training and inference cost comparable to state-of-the-art models. We hope that these results spark further research beyond the realms of well established CNNs and Transformers.[1]

## **1 Introduction**

As the history of computer vision demonstrates, the availability of larger datasets coupled with increased computational capacity often leads to a paradigm shift. While Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have been the de-facto standard for computer vision, recently Vision Transformers [14] (ViT), an alternative based on self-attention layers, attained state-of-the-art performance. ViT continues the long-lasting trend of removing hand-crafted visual features and inductive biases from models and relies further on learning from raw data.

We propose the _MLP-Mixer_ architecture (or “Mixer” for short), a competitive but conceptually and technically simple alternative, that does not use convolutions or self-attention. Instead, Mixer’s architecture is based entirely on multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) that are repeatedly applied across either spatial locations or feature channels. Mixer relies only on basic matrix multiplication routines, changes to data layout (reshapes and transpositions), and scalar nonlinearities.

Figure 1 depicts the macro-structure of Mixer. It accepts a sequence of linearly projected image patches (also referred to as _tokens_ ) shaped as a “patches _×_ channels” table as an input, and maintains this dimensionality. Mixer makes use of two types of MLP layers: _channel-mixing MLPs_ and _token-mixing MLPs_ . The channel-mixing MLPs allow communication between different channels;

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

Figure 1: MLP-Mixer consists of per-patch linear embeddings, Mixer layers, and a classifier head. Mixer layers contain one token-mixing MLP and one channel-mixing MLP, each consisting of two fully-connected layers and a GELU nonlinearity. they operate on each token independently and take individual rows of the table as inputs. The token-mixing MLPs allow communication between different spatial locations (tokens); they operate on each channel independently and take individual columns of the table as inputs. These two types of layers are interleaved to enable interaction of both input dimensions.

In the extreme case, our architecture can be seen as a very special CNN, which uses 1 _×_ 1 convolutions for _channel mixing_ , and single-channel depth-wise convolutions of a full receptive field and parameter sharing for _token mixing_ . However, the converse is not true as typical CNNs are not special cases of Mixer. Furthermore, a convolution is more complex than the plain matrix multiplication in MLPs as it requires an additional costly reduction to matrix multiplication and/or specialized implementation.

Despite its simplicity, Mixer attains competitive results. When pre-trained on large datasets (i.e., _∼_ 100M images), it reaches near state-of-the-art performance, previously claimed by CNNs and Transformers, in terms of the accuracy/cost trade-off. This includes 87.94% top-1 validation accuracy on ILSVRC2012 “ImageNet” [13]. When pre-trained on data of more modest scale (i.e., _∼_ 1– 10M images), coupled with modern regularization techniques [49, 54], Mixer also achieves strong performance. However, similar to ViT, it falls slightly short of specialized CNN architectures.

## **2 Mixer Architecture**

Modern deep vision architectures consist of layers that mix features (i) at a given spatial location, (ii) between different spatial locations, or both at once. In CNNs, (ii) is implemented with _N × N_ convolutions (for _N >_ 1) and pooling. Neurons in deeper layers have a larger receptive field [1, 29]. At the same time, 1 _×_ 1 convolutions also perform (i), and larger kernels perform both (i) and (ii). In Vision Transformers and other attention-based architectures, self-attention layers allow both (i) and (ii) and the MLP-blocks perform (i). The idea behind the Mixer architecture is to clearly separate the per-location ( _channel-mixing_ ) operations (i) and cross-location ( _token-mixing_ ) operations (ii). Both operations are implemented with MLPs. Figure 1 summarizes the architecture.

Mixer takes as input a sequence of _S_ non-overlapping image patches, each one projected to a desired _hidden dimension C_ . This results in a two-dimensional real-valued input table, **X** _∈_ R _[S][×][C]_ . If the original input image has resolution ( _H, W_ ), and each patch has resolution ( _P, P_ ), then the number of patches is _S_ = _HW/P_[2] . All patches are linearly projected with the _same_ projection matrix. Mixer consists of multiple layers of identical size, and each layer consists of two MLP blocks. The first one is the _token-mixing_ MLP: it acts on columns of **X** (i.e. it is applied to a transposed input table **X** _[⊤]_ ), maps R _[S] �→_ R _[S]_ , and is shared across all columns. The second one is the _channel-mixing_ MLP: it acts on rows of **X** , maps R _[C] �→_ R _[C]_ , and is shared across all rows. Each MLP block contains two

2

fully-connected layers and a nonlinearity applied independently to each row of its input data tensor. Mixer layers can be written as follows (omitting layer indices):

Here _σ_ is an element-wise nonlinearity (GELU [16]). _DS_ and _DC_ are tunable hidden widths in the token-mixing and channel-mixing MLPs, respectively. Note that _DS_ is selected independently of the number of input patches. Therefore, the computational complexity of the network is linear in the number of input patches, unlike ViT whose complexity is quadratic. Since _DC_ is independent of the patch size, the overall complexity is linear in the number of pixels in the image, as for a typical CNN.

As mentioned above, the _same_ channel-mixing MLP (token-mixing MLP) is applied to every row (column) of **X** . Tying the parameters of the channel-mixing MLPs (within each layer) is a natural choice—it provides positional invariance, a prominent feature of convolutions. However, tying parameters across channels is much less common. For example, separable convolutions [9, 40], used in some CNNs, apply convolutions to each channel independently of the other channels. However, in separable convolutions, a different convolutional kernel is applied to each channel unlike the token-mixing MLPs in Mixer that share the same kernel (of full receptive field) for all of the channels. The parameter tying prevents the architecture from growing too fast when increasing the hidden dimension _C_ or the sequence length _S_ and leads to significant memory savings. Surprisingly, this choice does not affect the empirical performance, see Supplementary A.1.

Each layer in Mixer (except for the initial patch projection layer) takes an input of the same size. This “isotropic” design is most similar to Transformers, or deep RNNs in other domains, that also use a fixed width. This is unlike most CNNs, which have a _pyramidal_ structure: deeper layers have a lower resolution input, but more channels. Note that while these are the typical designs, other combinations exist, such as isotropic ResNets [38] and pyramidal ViTs [52].

Aside from the MLP layers, Mixer uses other standard architectural components: skip-connections [15] and layer normalization [2]. Unlike ViTs, Mixer does not use position embeddings because the token-mixing MLPs are sensitive to the order of the input tokens. Finally, Mixer uses a standard classification head with the global average pooling layer followed by a linear classifier. Overall, the architecture can be written compactly in JAX/Flax, the code is given in Supplementary F.

## **3 Experiments**

We evaluate the performance of MLP-Mixer models, pre-trained with medium- to large-scale datasets, on a range of small and mid-sized downstream classification tasks. We are interested in three primary quantities: (1) Accuracy on the downstream task; (2) _Total_ computational cost of pre-training, which is important when training the model from scratch on the upstream dataset; (3) Test-time throughput, which is important to the practitioner. Our goal is not to demonstrate state-of-the-art results, but to show that, remarkably, a simple MLP-based model is competitive with today’s best convolutional and attention-based models.

**Downstream tasks** We use popular downstream tasks such as ILSVRC2012 “ImageNet” (1.3M training examples, 1k classes) with the original validation labels [13] and cleaned-up ReaL labels [5], CIFAR-10/100 (50k examples, 10/100 classes) [23], Oxford-IIIT Pets (3.7k examples, 36 classes) [33], and Oxford Flowers-102 (2k examples, 102 classes) [32]. We also use the Visual Task Adaptation Benchmark (VTAB-1k), which consists of 19 diverse datasets, each with 1k training examples [58].

**Pre-training** We follow the standard transfer learning setup: pre-training followed by fine-tuning on the downstream tasks. We pre-train our models on two public datasets: ILSVRC2021 ImageNet, and ImageNet-21k, a superset of ILSVRC2012 that contains 21k classes and 14M images [13]. To assess performance at larger scale, we also train on JFT-300M, a proprietary dataset with 300M examples and 18k classes [44]. We de-duplicate all pre-training datasets with respect to the test sets of the downstream tasks as done in Dosovitskiy et al. [14], Kolesnikov et al. [22]. We pre-train all models at resolution 224 using Adam with _β_ 1 = 0 _._ 9, _β_ 2 = 0 _._ 999, linear learning rate warmup of 10k steps and linear decay, batch size 4 096, weight decay, and gradient clipping at global norm 1. For JFT-300M, we pre-process images by applying the cropping technique from Szegedy et al. [45] in addition to random horizontal flipping. For ImageNet and ImageNet-21k, we employ additional data augmentation and regularization techniques. In particular, we use RandAugment [12], mixup [60],

**Fine-tuning** We fine-tune using momentum SGD, batch size 512, gradient clipping at global norm 1, and a cosine learning rate schedule with a linear warmup. We do not use weight decay when finetuning. Following common practice [22, 48], we also fine-tune at higher resolutions with respect to those used during pre-training. Since we keep the patch resolution fixed, this increases the number of input patches (say from _S_ to _S[′]_ ) and thus requires modifying the shape of Mixer’s token-mixing MLP blocks. Formally, the input in Eq. (1) is left-multiplied by a weight matrix **W** 1 _∈_ R _[D][S][×][S]_ and this operation has to be adjusted when changing the input dimension _S_ . For this, we increase the hidden layer width from _DS_ to _DS′_ in proportion to the number of patches and initialize the (now larger) weight matrix **W** 2 _[′][∈]_[R] _[D][S][′][×][S][′]_[with a block-diagonal matrix containing copies of] **[ W]**[2][on its] diagonal. This particular scheme only allows for _S[′]_ = _K_[2] _S_ with _K ∈_ N. See Supplementary C for further details. On the VTAB-1k benchmark we follow the BiT-HyperRule [22] and fine-tune Mixer models at resolution 224 and 448 on the datasets with small and large input images respectively.

**Metrics** We evaluate the trade-off between the model’s computational cost and quality. For the former we compute two metrics: (1) Total pre-training time on TPU-v3 accelerators, which combines three relevant factors: the theoretical FLOPs for each training setup, the computational efficiency on the relevant training hardware, and the data efficiency. (2) Throughput in images/sec/core on TPU-v3. Since models of different sizes may benefit from different batch sizes, we sweep the batch sizes and report the highest throughput for each model. For model quality, we focus on top-1 downstream accuracy after fine-tuning. On two occasions (Figure 3, right and Figure 4), where fine-tuning all of the models is too costly, we report the few-shot accuracies obtained by solving the _ℓ_ 2-regularized linear regression problem between the frozen learned representations of images and the labels.

**Models** We compare various configurations of Mixer, summarized in Table 1, to the most recent, state-of-the-art, CNNs and attention-based models. In all the figures and tables, the MLP-based Mixer models are marked with pink ( ), convolution-based models with yellow ( ), and attention-based models with blue ( ). The Vision Transformers (ViTs) have model scales and patch resolutions similar to Mixer. HaloNets are attention-based models that use a ResNet-like structure with local selfattention layers instead of 3 _×_ 3 convolutions [51]. We focus on the particularly efficient “HaloNet-H4 (base 128, Conv-12)” model, which is a hybrid variant of the wider HaloNet-H4 architecture with some of the self-attention layers replaced by convolutions. Note, we mark HaloNets with both attention and convolutions with blue ( ). Big Transfer (BiT) [22] models are ResNets optimized for transfer learning. NFNets [7] are normalizer-free ResNets with several optimizations for ImageNet classification. We consider the NFNet-F4+ model variant. We consider MPL [35] and ALIGN [21] for EfficientNet architectures. MPL is pre-trained at very large-scale on JFT-300M images, using meta-pseudo labelling from ImageNet instead of the original labels. We compare to the EfficientNetB6-Wide model variant. ALIGN pre-train image encoder and language encoder on noisy web image text pairs in a contrastive way. We compare to their best EfficientNet-L2 image encoder.

labels. “Avg. 5” stands for the average performance across all five downstream tasks (ImageNet, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, Pets, Flowers). Figure 2 (left) visualizes the accuracy-compute frontier. When pre-trained on ImageNet-21k with additional regularization, Mixer achieves an overall strong performance (84.15% top-1 on ImageNet), although slightly inferior to other models[2] . Regularization in this scenario is necessary and Mixer overfits without it, which is consistent with similar observations for ViT [14]. The same conclusion holds when training Mixer from random initialization on ImageNet (see Section 3.2): Mixer-B/16 attains a reasonable score of 76.4% at resolution 224, but tends to overfit. This score is similar to a vanilla ResNet50, but behind state-of-the-art CNNs/hybrids for the ImageNet “from scratch” setting, e.g. 84.7% BotNet [42] and 86.5% NFNet [7].

When the size of the upstream dataset increases, Mixer’s performance improves significantly. In particular, Mixer-H/14 achieves 87.94% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet, which is 0.5% better than BiTResNet152x4 and only 0.5% lower than ViT-H/14. Remarkably, Mixer-H/14 runs 2.5 times faster than ViT-H/14 and almost twice as fast as BiT. Overall, Figure 2 (left) supports our main claim that in terms of the accuracy-compute trade-off Mixer is competitive with more conventional neural network architectures. The figure also demonstrates a clear correlation between the total pre-training cost and the downstream accuracy, even across architecture classes.

BiT-ResNet152x4 in the table are pre-trained using SGD with momentum and a long schedule. Since Adam tends to converge faster, we complete the picture in Figure 2 (left) with the BiT-R200x3 model from Dosovitskiy et al. [14] pre-trained on JFT-300M using Adam. This ResNet has a slightly lower accuracy, but considerably lower pre-training compute. Finally, the results of smaller ViT-L/16 and Mixer-L/16 models are also reported in this figure.

## **3.2 The role of the model scale**

The results outlined in the previous section focus on (large) models at the upper end of the compute spectrum. We now turn our attention to smaller Mixer models.
We may scale the model in two independent ways: (1) Increasing the model size (number of layers, hidden dimension, MLP widths) when pre-training; (2) Increasing the input image resolution when

We compare various configurations of Mixer (see Table 1) to ViT models of similar scales and BiT models pre-trained with Adam. The results are summarized in Table 3 and Figure 3. When trained from scratch on ImageNet, Mixer-B/16 achieves a reasonable top-1 accuracy of 76.44%. This is 3% behind the ViT-B/16 model. The training curves (not reported) reveal that both models achieve very similar values of the training loss. In other words, Mixer-B/16 overfits more than ViT-B/16. For the Mixer-L/16 and ViT-L/16 models this difference is even more pronounced.

As the pre-training dataset grows, Mixer’s performance steadily improves. Remarkably, Mixer-H/14 pre-trained on JFT-300M and fine-tuned at 224 resolution is only 0.3% behind ViT-H/14 on ImageNet whilst running 2.2 times faster. Figure 3 clearly demonstrates that although Mixer is slightly below the frontier on the lower end of model scales, it sits confidently on the frontier at the high end.

## **3.3 The role of the pre-training dataset size**

The results presented thus far demonstrate that pre-training on larger datasets significantly improves Mixer’s performance. Here, we study this effect in more detail.

To study Mixer’s ability to make use of the growing number of training examples we pre-train Mixer-B/32, Mixer-L/32, and Mixer-L/16 models on random subsets of JFT-300M containing 3%, 10%, 30% and 100% of all the training examples for 233, 70, 23, and 7 epochs. Thus, every model is pre-trained for the same number of total steps. We also pre-train Mixer-L/16 model on an even larger JFT-3B dataset [59] containing roughly 3B images with 30k classes for the same number of total steps.

While not strictly comparable, this allows us to further extrapolate the effect of scale. We use the linear 5-shot top-1 accuracy on ImageNet as a proxy for transfer quality. For every pre-training run we perform early stopping based on the best upstream validation performance. Results are reported in Figure 2 (right), where we also include ViT-B/32, ViT-L/32, ViT-L/16, and BiT-R152x2 models.

When pre-trained on the smallest subset of JFT-300M, all Mixer models strongly overfit. BiT models also overfit, but to a lesser extent, possibly due to the strong inductive biases associated with the convolutions. As the dataset increases, the performance of both Mixer-L/32 and Mixer-L/16 grows faster than BiT; Mixer-L/16 keeps improving, while the BiT model plateaus.

The same conclusions hold for ViT, consistent with Dosovitskiy et al. [14]. However, the relative improvement of larger Mixer models are even more pronounced. The performance gap between Mixer-L/16 and ViT-L/16 shrinks with data scale. It appears that Mixer benefits from the growing dataset size even more than ViT. One could speculate and explain it again with the difference in inductive biases: self-attention layers in ViT lead to certain properties of the learned functions that are _less compatible_ with the true underlying distribution than those discovered with Mixer architecture.

## **3.5 Visualization**

It is commonly observed that the first layers of CNNs tend to learn Gabor-like detectors that act on pixels in local regions of the image. In contrast, Mixer allows for global information exchange in the token-mixing MLPs, which begs the question whether it processes information in a similar fashion. Figure 5 shows hidden units of the first three token-mixing MLPs of Mixer trained on JFT-300M. Recall that the token-mixing MLPs allow global communication between different spatial locations. Some of the learned features operate on the entire image, while others operate on smaller regions. Deeper layers appear to have no clearly identifiable structure. Similar to CNNs, we observe many pairs of feature detectors with opposite phases [39]. The structure of learned units depends on the hyperparameters. Plots for the first embedding layer appear in Figure 2 of Supplementary D.

8

## **4 Related work**

MLP-Mixer is a new architecture for computer vision that differs from previous successful architectures because it uses neither convolutional nor self-attention layers. Nevertheless, the design choices can be traced back to ideas from the literature on CNNs [24, 25] and Transformers [50].

CNNs have been the de-facto standard in computer vision since the AlexNet model [24] surpassed prevailing approaches based on hand-crafted image features [36]. Many works focused on improving the design of CNNs. Simonyan and Zisserman [41] demonstrated that one can train state-of-the-art models using only convolutions with small 3 _×_ 3 kernels. He et al. [15] introduced skip-connections together with the batch normalization [20], which enabled training of very deep neural networks and further improved performance. A prominent line of research has investigated the benefits of using sparse convolutions, such as grouped [57] or depth-wise [9, 17] variants. In a similar spirit to our token-mixing MLPs, Wu et al. [55] share parameters in the depth-wise convolutions for natural language processing. Hu et al. [18] and Wang et al. [53] propose to augment convolutional networks with non-local operations to partially alleviate the constraint of local processing from CNNs. Mixer takes the idea of using convolutions with small kernels to the extreme: by reducing the kernel size to 1 _×_ 1 it turns convolutions into standard dense matrix multiplications applied independently to each spatial location (channel-mixing MLPs). This alone does not allow aggregation of spatial information and to compensate we apply dense matrix multiplications that are applied to every feature across all spatial locations (token-mixing MLPs). In Mixer, matrix multiplications are applied row-wise or column-wise on the “patches _×_ features” input table, which is also closely related to the work on sparse convolutions. Mixer uses skip-connections [15] and normalization layers [2, 20].

In computer vision, self-attention based Transformer architectures were initially applied for generative modeling [8, 34]. Their value for image recognition was demonstrated later, albeit in combination with a convolution-like locality bias [37], or on low-resolution images [10]. Dosovitskiy et al. [14] introduced ViT, a pure transformer model that has fewer locality biases, but scales well to large data. ViT achieves state-of-the-art performance on popular vision benchmarks while retaining the robustness of CNNs [6]. Touvron et al. [49] trained ViT effectively on smaller datasets using extensive regularization. Mixer borrows design choices from recent transformer-based architectures. The design of Mixer’s MLP-blocks originates in [27, 50]. Converting images to a sequence of patches and directly processing embeddings of these patches originates in Dosovitskiy et al. [14].

Many recent works strive to design more effective architectures for vision. Srinivas et al. [42] replace 3 _×_ 3 convolutions in ResNets by self-attention layers. Ramachandran et al. [37], Tay et al. [47], Li et al. [26], and Bello [3] design networks with new attention-like mechanisms. Mixer can be seen as a step in an orthogonal direction, without reliance on locality bias and attention mechanisms.

The work of Lin et al. [28] is closely related. It attains reasonable performance on CIFAR-10 using fully connected networks, heavy data augmentation, and pre-training with an auto-encoder. Neyshabur [31] devises custom regularization and optimization algorithms and trains a fully-connected network, attaining impressive performance on small-scale tasks. Instead we rely on token and channel-mixing MLPs, use standard regularization and optimization techniques, and scale to large data effectively.

Traditionally, networks evaluated on ImageNet [13] are trained from random initialization using Inception-style pre-processing [46]. For smaller datasets, transfer of ImageNet models is popular. However, modern state-of-the-art models typically use either weights pre-trained on larger datasets, or more recent data-augmentation and training strategies. For example, Dosovitskiy et al. [14], Kolesnikov et al. [22], Mahajan et al. [30], Pham et al. [35], Xie et al. [56] all advance state-of-the-art in image classification using large-scale pre-training. Examples of improvements due to augmentation or regularization changes include Cubuk et al. [11], who attain excellent classification performance with learned data augmentation, and Bello et al. [4], who show that canonical ResNets are still near state-of-the-art, if one uses recent training and augmentation strategies.

## **5 Conclusions**

We describe a very simple architecture for vision. Our experiments demonstrate that it is as good as existing state-of-the-art methods in terms of the trade-off between accuracy and computational resources required for training and inference. We believe these results open many questions. On the practical side, it may be useful to study the features learned by the model and identify the main

9

differences (if any) from those learned by CNNs and Transformers. On the theoretical side, we would like to understand the inductive biases hidden in these various features and eventually their role in generalization. Most of all, we hope that our results spark further research, beyond the realms of established models based on convolutions and self-attention. It would be particularly interesting to see whether such a design works in NLP or other domains.

We thank Josip Djolonga for feedback on the initial version of the paper; Preetum Nakkiran for proposing to train MLP-Mixer on input images with shuffled pixels; Olivier Bousquet, Yann Dauphin, and Dirk Weissenborn for useful discussions.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces MLP-Mixer, an architecture that replaces convolutions and self-attention with alternating channel-mixing and token-mixing MLPs. The work provides a compelling demonstration that pure MLPs can achieve competitive classification performance on large-scale datasets. However, the manuscript contains fundamental theoretical inaccuracies regarding permutation invariance and computational complexity that require correction. The evaluation also lacks standard downstream benchmarks and overstates practical utility given the model's heavy reliance on massive pre-training corpora.

MLP-Mixer offers a conceptually simple and empirically strong alternative to convolutional and attention-based vision models, successfully challenging the necessity of locality and attention inductive biases. The authors effectively demonstrate scaling laws and the benefits of large-scale pre-training. The primary weakness lies in theoretical inaccuracies and a narrow evaluation scope that overlooks critical practical constraints.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical Error on Permutation Invariance and Positional Encodings: Section 2 states that "Unlike ViTs, Mixer does not use position embeddings because the token-mixing MLPs are sensitive to the order of the input tokens." This claim is mathematically incorrect. Standard fully connected layers operate on sets and are inherently permutation-invariant across their input dimension. Applying a shared weight matrix to each column of the patch-feature table does not introduce order sensitivity. If the model truly lacks positional encodings, it treats images as unordered bags of patches, which severely limits spatial reasoning and contradicts the reported accuracy. The authors must clarify whether positional information was injected and update the text accordingly. This correction is essential to maintain theoretical rigor.

- Incorrect Complexity Analysis: The paper asserts in Section 2 that the computational complexity is linear in the number of input patches, explicitly contrasting this with the quadratic complexity of Vision Transformers. This assertion is flawed. A token-mixing MLP that maps $\mathbb{R}^S \to \mathbb{R}^S$ using a dense weight matrix requires $O(S^2)$ operations per token channel. Since the network processes $C$ channels, the total complexity scales as $O(C S^2)$, which matches the quadratic scaling of self-attention. Claiming linear complexity misrepresents the model's scalability and could mislead practitioners evaluating it for high-resolution vision tasks. The authors should revise the complexity derivation to accurately reflect the quadratic dependence on sequence length and discuss the practical implications for processing large images.

- Overstated Practical Utility vs. Data Hunger: The abstract and introduction frame MLP-Mixer as a competitive alternative to established CNNs and Transformers, yet the experimental results reveal a heavy dependence on massive pre-training corpora and aggressive regularization. Table 2 and Section 3.2 show that Mixer significantly underperforms ResNets and ViTs on standard ImageNet-1k or smaller datasets unless pre-trained on ImageNet-21k or JFT-300M with techniques like mixup and RandAugment. The paper acknowledges this data hunger but minimizes its impact on the model's practical viability. Convolutional architectures retain a clear advantage in data efficiency due to their locality inductive biases. The authors should temper the claims of broad competitiveness and explicitly quantify the data efficiency gap to provide a realistic assessment of when MLP-Mixer is a suitable choice.

- Missing Dense Prediction Benchmarks: The evaluation focuses exclusively on image classification and linear probing on VTAB-1k classification subsets. Modern vision architectures are routinely assessed on detection and segmentation tasks, where hierarchical feature extraction and spatial locality are paramount. The paper does not report results on COCO, Pascal VOC, or ADE20K, leaving a critical gap in the analysis. Pure MLP architectures lack the built-in spatial hierarchy of CNNs, which typically leads to performance degradation on dense prediction tasks. Including at least a baseline comparison on a segmentation benchmark would demonstrate whether the token-mixing mechanism can effectively capture multi-scale spatial dependencies or if it fundamentally struggles outside of classification.

- Rigid Resolution Scaling Mechanism: Section 3 describes a specific procedure for adapting the model to different fine-tuning resolutions by resizing the token-mixing MLP weights using a block-diagonal initialization scheme. This approach strictly requires the new sequence length to satisfy $S' = K^2 S$, severely limiting flexibility during transfer learning. Vision tasks frequently demand multi-scale training or arbitrary input dimensions, which this constraint makes cumbersome. The paper mentions this workaround briefly but fails to analyze its impact on downstream performance or compare it to the seamless resolution handling of convolutions and attention. The authors should expand the discussion to address how this rigidity affects practical deployment and consider whether alternative interpolation or projection strategies could mitigate the limitation.

- Ablation isolating token-mixing and channel-mixing contributions: The paper’s central architectural claim relies on the interleaving of token-mixing and channel-mixing MLPs. Yet it never isolates their individual effects. Without removing one of these components, readers cannot verify that both are strictly necessary for the reported performance gains. This omission leaves a gap in validating the proposed design principle. I recommend adding a controlled ablation study on ImageNet-1k or CIFAR-100 where token-mixing is disabled. This forces independent classification per patch. Comparing these variants against the full Mixer would concretely demonstrate the contribution of each mixing mechanism.

- Quantitative analysis of spatial dependency capture: The authors contrast Mixer with CNNs and ViTs using qualitative feature visualizations and permutation invariance tests. Qualitative plots alone do not substantiate claims about the model's inductive biases. A quantitative assessment would ground the discussion in Section 3.5 and strengthen the comparison to established architectures. Compute a spatial locality metric, such as the average mutual information between patch embeddings or the effective receptive field size, for Mixer, ViT, and a ResNet on a held-out validation set. Reporting these values alongside the existing figures would clarify how Mixer balances local and global context.

- High-resolution computational cost analysis: The experimental evaluation stops at fine-tuning resolutions of 448 pixels. This leaves the practical scalability of the architecture untested at higher resolutions. Since the token-mixing MLPs scale quadratically with the sequence length, increasing the resolution to 896 or 1024 pixels will drastically increase memory usage and inference latency. This gap matters because the paper heavily emphasizes the accuracy-compute trade-off as a primary contribution. Provide a runtime and memory profiling analysis for fine-tuning at resolutions 448, 896, and 1024. Include throughput measurements to show exactly where the quadratic scaling becomes a bottleneck compared to convolutional baselines. Discuss whether patch-merging strategies could mitigate the cost.

Recommendation: Major revision. The manuscript contains fundamental theoretical inaccuracies regarding permutation invariance and computational complexity that directly contradict the architecture's mathematical properties. Additionally, the evaluation lacks standard downstream benchmarks and overstates practical utility given the model's heavy reliance on massive datasets.

Revision targets:

- Correct the theoretical claims about permutation invariance and positional embeddings in Section 2, and clarify whether positional information was actually used during training.

- Revise the complexity analysis to accurately reflect quadratic scaling with sequence length and discuss the practical implications for high-resolution processing.

- Add ablation studies or baseline comparisons on detection and segmentation benchmarks to evaluate the architecture's spatial reasoning capabilities beyond classification.

- Temper claims of broad competitiveness by explicitly quantifying the data efficiency gap compared to CNNs and ViTs, and discuss the practical constraints of the resolution scaling mechanism.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of resolution scaling impact on compute
comments.quote: While the former affects both pre-training compute and test-time throughput, the latter only affects the throughput.
comments.explanation: The claim that increasing input image resolution 'only affects the throughput' is inaccurate for the Mixer architecture. The token-mixing MLP applies a dense weight matrix across the sequence of image patches, resulting in a computational complexity that scales quadratically with the sequence length. Increasing the fine-tuning resolution directly increases the number of patches, which proportionally increases the FLOPs required for both pre-training and inference. Correct this statement to reflect the quadratic dependence of token-mixing operations on sequence length.

comments.title: Contradiction between fixed steps and early stopping
comments.quote: Thus, every model is pre-trained for the same number of total steps.
comments.explanation: This statement directly contradicts a later claim in the same section: 'For every pre-training run we perform early stopping based on the best upstream validation performance.' If models are trained for a fixed number of steps, early stopping cannot be applied based on validation performance, as that would result in varying step counts. Clarify the exact training protocol to resolve this logical inconsistency.

comments.title: Incorrect terminology for transfer evaluation metric
comments.quote: We use the linear 5-shot top-1 accuracy on ImageNet as a proxy for transfer quality.
comments.explanation: The term '5-shot' refers to a few-shot learning setting with only five examples per class, which contradicts standard transfer learning evaluations and the 'ImNet top-1' metric reported in Table 3. This is almost certainly a typographical error. Replace '5-shot' with 'probing' or 'fine-tuning' to match the experimental setup and table headers.

comments.title: Global MLP weights mischaracterized as regional features
comments.quote: Some of the learned features operate on the entire image, while others operate on smaller regions.
comments.explanation: Dense token-mixing MLPs apply a full weight matrix across all patches, meaning their architectural receptive field is strictly global. Describing learned units as operating on 'smaller regions' implies a locality bias that the architecture does not possess. The authors likely refer to patterns in the weight magnitudes where certain neurons assign higher importance to specific patches. Rewrite this sentence to clarify that while weight magnitudes may vary, the mixing mechanism itself is global and lacks locality.

comments.title: Inaccurate analogy equating token-mixing MLPs to depth-wise convolutions
comments.quote:
sions.

In the extreme case, our architecture can be seen as a very special CNN, which uses  $1 \times 1$  convolutions for channel mixing, and single-channel depth-wise convolutions of a full receptive field and parameter sharing for token mixing. However, the
comments.explanation: The comparison to a depth-wise convolution is mathematically imprecise. A depth-wise convolution applies a filter that slides across spatial positions with weight sharing, which enforces locality. The token-mixing MLP in Mixer applies a dense weight matrix independently to each channel across all patches, meaning it lacks both spatial locality and spatial weight-sharing. Equating a global dense layer to a convolution obscures the fundamental difference in inductive bias. Rewrite the passage to describe token mixing as 'per-channel dense layers applied across the sequence dimension' rather than depth-wise convolutions.

comments.title: Oxford Flowers-102 example count

comments.explanation: The dataset actually contains 8,189 images, not 2,000. The '2k' figure is likely a typo for '8k'. Updating this ensures accurate reporting of the experimental scale.

comments.explanation: The paragraph introduces the first token-mixing weight matrix as W1, but then abruptly switches to discussing W2 and W2' with the exact same dimensions (R^{D_{S'} x S'}). comments.title: Caption sorting claim contradicts table layout

comments.title: Throughput comparison overstates speed advantage

While 40/15 ≈ 2.67 supports the first claim, 40/26 ≈ 1.54 does not justify 'almost twice as fast.' This phrasing exaggerates the speed advantage over BiT. comments.title: Identical symbols used for different model types in legend
comments.quote: s. In all the figures and tables, the MLP-based Mixer models are marked with pink  $(\bullet)$ , convolution-based models with yellow  $(\circ)$ , and attention-based models with blue  $(\bullet)$ . T

comments.title: Unexplained mechanism for permutation invariance
comments.quote: As could be expected, Mixer is invariant to the order of patches and pixels within the patches (the blue and green curves match perfectly).
comments.explanation: The claim of invariance is mathematically sound but the text omits the architectural component that guarantees it. The token-mixing MLP applies a dense weight matrix across the sequence dimension, which permutes the output features when the input order changes. The invariance only holds because the model applies permutation-invariant pooling before the final classification head. Add a clause specifying that 'mean pooling over the patch sequence ensures this invariance' to close the logical gap.

comments.title: Dense matrix multiplications incorrectly linked to sparse convolutions
comments.quote: s). In Mixer, matrix multiplications are applied row-wise or column-wise on the “patches$\times$features” input table, which is also closely related to the work on sparse convolutions. Mixer
comments.explanation: This comparison is conceptually mismatched. Sparse convolutions are designed to reduce computational cost by exploiting sparsity in the input or weight matrices. Mixer’s token- and channel-mixing layers rely on dense matrix multiplications, which are computationally heavier and lack the sparsity-inducing properties of the cited works. The connection is tenuous and may confuse readers about the model's efficiency characteristics. Remove this sentence to prevent conceptual confusion.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
MLP-Mixer introduces a vision architecture relying exclusively on multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) for both channel and token mixing, challenging the necessity of convolutions and self-attention in high-performance vision models.

The paper provides a compelling, well-executed empirical study that successfully demonstrates the viability of all-MLP architectures for large-scale image classification. The architecture's simplicity and competitive performance on massive datasets represent a significant contribution to the field, effectively questioning the ubiquity of inductive biases found in CNNs and Transformers.

Overview issues:

- Dependence on Massive Pre-training Data: The authors acknowledge that MLP-Mixer significantly underperforms compared to CNNs when trained from scratch on smaller datasets like ImageNet-1K, as seen in Section 3.1. The paper relies heavily on JFT-300M and ImageNet-21K to mask this architectural weakness. This suggests that the model lacks the necessary inductive biases to learn efficiently from limited data, which limits its utility for researchers without access to massive compute and proprietary datasets. The revision should include a more thorough investigation into whether specific architectural tweaks or regularization strategies can bridge this gap for smaller data regimes.

- Sensitivity to Input Resolution and Scaling: Section 3.1 and 3.2 discuss fine-tuning at higher resolutions, but the mechanism for modifying the token-mixing MLP weight matrix (via block-diagonal expansion) is only briefly mentioned in the text and relegated to the Supplementary Material. Because the token-mixing MLP is tied to the sequence length S, changing resolution fundamentally alters the model's capacity and learned spatial interactions. A more detailed analysis of how this scaling affects the model's learned features—and whether this 'patch-stretching' is truly optimal—is required to understand the model's flexibility compared to the translation-invariant nature of standard convolutions.

- Lack of Robustness Analysis: While the paper evaluates accuracy and throughput, it ignores the robustness of MLP-Mixer to common corruptions or adversarial attacks. Given that Transformers and CNNs have distinct robustness profiles due to their differing inductive biases, the pure MLP design warrants a specific evaluation. If the model is to be considered a viable alternative to CNNs, practitioners need to know if the lack of spatial inductive bias makes it more susceptible to adversarial perturbations or distribution shifts. Including a comparison against standard benchmarks like ImageNet-C would significantly strengthen the paper's claims.

- Efficiency Claims vs. Hardware Implementation: The paper claims that MLP-Mixer is efficient because it relies on matrix multiplication, yet the throughput metrics in Table 2 are highly dependent on TPU-specific optimizations. The authors note that convolutions require costly reduction to matrix multiplication (im2col), but they do not account for the potential for highly optimized hardware-specific kernels for convolutions that might not exist for the specific reshapes/transpositions required by Mixer. A more transparent discussion of the memory overhead associated with the large intermediate activations in MLP-Mixer would be beneficial, as these can often become a bottleneck in memory-constrained environments.

- Absence of empirical evidence for scaling laws beyond JFT-300M: The paper claims that MLP-Mixer improves more rapidly with data than ResNets or ViTs, yet this is only demonstrated up to the JFT-300M scale. Given the central argument that Mixer is a viable alternative to CNNs and Transformers, it is essential to show this scaling behavior holds at the JFT-3B scale mentioned in the text. The paper should include a plot comparing the performance of Mixer-H/14 against a comparable ViT-H/14 at the 3B image scale. Without this, the claim that Mixer benefits from growing dataset size more than ViT remains a speculation rather than a demonstrated property of the architecture.

- Lack of analysis on the 'patch-stretching' initialization stability: The authors describe a specific block-diagonal initialization scheme to adapt the token-mixing MLP to higher resolutions during fine-tuning. This procedure is critical for the model's flexibility, yet the paper provides no evidence regarding the stability of this initialization or how it affects the convergence rate compared to training from scratch at the target resolution. A small-scale experiment comparing the fine-tuning accuracy of the block-diagonal initialization against a random initialization of the expanded weights would clarify whether this architectural choice is a necessary inductive bias or simply a heuristic for avoiding training failure.

- Missing comparison of memory footprint for high-resolution inference: The paper emphasizes throughput as a primary metric but ignores the peak memory consumption of the model at higher resolutions. Because the token-mixing MLP operates on a sequence length S that grows quadratically with resolution, the memory cost of the intermediate activations in the Mixer blocks may scale differently than in standard CNNs. A plot showing memory usage vs. input resolution for Mixer-L/16 compared to a standard ResNet-152 would provide practitioners with the necessary guidance to assess if the architecture is truly efficient for high-resolution tasks or if it hits memory bottlenecks that the throughput metrics mask.

Recommendation: accept

Revision targets:

- Provide a more comprehensive analysis of the model's performance on smaller datasets, including potential regularization techniques that could mitigate the observed overfitting.

- Include a robustness evaluation (e.g., ImageNet-C or adversarial robustness) to better characterize the model's behavior under non-ideal data conditions.

- Expand the discussion on the memory overhead and hardware-specific performance bottlenecks to provide a more balanced view of the 'efficiency' claims.

comments.title: Inconsistent claim regarding performance scaling
comments.quote: It appears that Mixer benefits from the growing dataset size even more than ViT. One could speculate and explain it again with the difference in inductive biases: self-attention layers in ViT lead to certain properties of the learned functions that are less compatible with the true underlying distribution than those discovered with Mixer architecture.
comments.explanation: The assertion that Mixer scales better than ViT is not supported by the provided data. In Table 3, comparing Mixer-L/16 and ViT-L/16 at 14 epochs on JFT-300M, the ViT model achieves 85.63% top-1 accuracy while the Mixer model achieves 84.82%. Without a direct comparison at the JFT-3B scale or a rigorous analysis of the performance slopes, this claim is speculative and contradicts the table values where ViT remains the superior performer at equivalent compute budgets. Please temper this claim or provide the specific scaling metrics that justify it.

comments.title: Inconsistent claim regarding state-of-the-art performance
comments.quote: Our experiments demonstrate that it is as good as existing state-of-the-art methods in terms of the trade-off between accuracy and computational resources required for training and inference.
comments.explanation: This conclusion is overly broad and contradicts the findings in Section 3.1, where the authors acknowledge that MLP-Mixer underperforms compared to CNNs and ViTs when trained from scratch on smaller datasets. The model's competitiveness is conditional on massive pre-training (JFT-300M/ImageNet-21K). Please qualify this statement to reflect that the architecture's parity with state-of-the-art methods is dependent on the large-scale pre-training regime.

comments.title: Ambiguity in LayerNorm application
comments.quote:
$\mathbf{U}_{*,i}$ $=\mathbf{X}_{*,i}+\mathbf{W}_{2}\,\sigma\big{(}\mathbf{W}_{1}\,\text{LayerNorm}(\mathbf{X})_{*,i}\big{)},\quad\text{ for }i=1\dots C,$ (1)
$\mathbf{Y}_{j,*}$ $=\mathbf{U}_{j,*}+\mathbf{W}_{4}\,\sigma\big{(}\mathbf{W}_{3}\,\text{LayerNorm}(\mathbf{U})_{j,*}\big{)},\quad\text{ for }j=1\dots S.$
comments.explanation: The notation $\text{LayerNorm}(\mathbf{X})_{*,i}$ is ambiguous regarding the normalization axes. Standard LayerNorm in Transformers typically normalizes over the feature dimension, but in the Mixer architecture, applying this to the token-mixing block (which operates on columns) might imply normalization over the sequence dimension $S$ or the channel dimension $C$. Please explicitly define the normalization axes for both the token-mixing and channel-mixing blocks to ensure reproducibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent throughput comparison for Mixer-H/14
comments.quote: Remarkably, Mixer-H/14 runs 2.5 times faster than ViT-H/14 and almost twice as fast as BiT.
comments.explanation: The claim that Mixer-H/14 runs 'almost twice as fast' as BiT-R152x4 is not supported by Table 2. The table lists the throughput for Mixer-H/14 as 40 img/sec/core and for BiT-R152x4 as 26 img/sec/core. The ratio is approximately 1.54, not 2.0. Please correct the multiplier to accurately reflect the provided throughput metrics.

comments.title: Ambiguity in token-mixing MLP weight initialization
comments.quote: For this, we increase the hidden layer width from  $D_S$  to  $D_{S'}$  in proportion to the number of patches and initialize the (now larger) weight matrix  $\mathbf{W}_2' \in \mathbb{R}^{D_{S'} \times S'}$  with a block-diagonal matrix containing copies of  $\mathbf{W}_2$  on its diagonal.
comments.explanation: The text states that the hidden layer width $D_S$ is increased to $D_{S'}$ in proportion to the number of patches, but $D_S$ is defined as a fixed hyperparameter for a given model scale in Table 1. If $D_S$ is scaled, it changes the model's capacity and contradicts the definition of fixed hidden dimensions. Please clarify if $D_S$ is being scaled or if this is a misstatement regarding the expansion of the sequence dimension $S$ only.

comments.title: Inaccurate description of token-mixing MLP parameter sharing
comments.quote: In the extreme case, our architecture can be seen as a very special CNN, which uses  $1 \times 1$  convolutions for channel mixing, and single-channel depth-wise convolutions of a full receptive field and parameter sharing for token
comments.explanation: The claim that token-mixing MLPs use parameter sharing across the entire receptive field is imprecise. The token-mixing MLP performs a dense linear transformation on the flattened spatial dimension; it is not a convolution. Describing it as a convolution with a 'full receptive field' is misleading because the MLP is a global operator on the sequence of tokens, whereas a convolution is a local operator. Please clarify that the parameter sharing occurs across channels, not across spatial locations in the convolutional sense.

comments.title: Inaccurate description of VTAB-1k dataset composition
comments.quote:
2]*. We also use the Visual Task Adaptation Benchmark (VTAB-1k), which consists of 19 diverse datasets, each with 1k training examples *[58]*.

####
comments.explanation: Describing VTAB-1k as consisting of 19 datasets, each with 1k training examples, is technically imprecise. The '1k' refers to the training set constraint, not the dataset size itself, and the number of classes varies significantly across the 19 tasks. It would be more accurate to state that the benchmark evaluates transfer learning performance using a restricted training set of 1,000 examples per task.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a novel architecture that challenges the necessity of convolutions and self-attention in vision models, demonstrating competitive performance at scale. Its main strength is the conceptual simplicity and thorough empirical evaluation across multiple datasets and model sizes. However, the paper has several significant issues regarding the interpretation of results, the fairness of comparisons, and the depth of analysis supporting its claims.

Overview issues:

- Unfair comparison with ViT on small data regimes: The paper reports that Mixer-B/16 achieves 76.44% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet from scratch, which is 3% behind ViT-B/16. However, the paper does not control for training hyperparameters or regularization strength between the two architectures. The claim that 'Mixer overfits more than ViT' is based on a single training setup, but it is well-known that different architectures respond differently to the same regularization. A proper comparison would require tuning regularization hyperparameters separately for each model to achieve optimal performance. The paper should include an ablation where both models are trained with matched regularization budgets and learning rate schedules, or at least acknowledge that the comparison is not controlled.

- Lack of rigorous ablation on the role of positional information: The paper claims that Mixer does not need position embeddings because token-mixing MLPs are sensitive to input order, but this claim is not rigorously tested. Section 3.4 shows that Mixer is invariant to patch permutations when trained with shuffled patches, but this does not demonstrate that the model learns positional information when trained on natural images. The paper should include an experiment comparing Mixer with and without explicit position embeddings on standard ImageNet training, measuring whether performance changes. Without this, the claim that position embeddings are unnecessary is unsupported.

- Insufficient analysis of token-mixing MLP features: Figure 5 shows learned token-mixing weights but provides no quantitative analysis of what these features represent. The paper states that 'some features operate on the entire image, while others operate on smaller regions,' but this is a qualitative observation. The paper would benefit from a systematic analysis, such as measuring the effective receptive field of token-mixing MLPs, or comparing the frequency content of learned filters to those in CNNs. Without such analysis, the claim that Mixer processes information differently from CNNs remains speculative.

- Missing comparison on computational efficiency at inference time: The paper reports throughput in images/sec/core but does not report FLOPs for each model. Throughput can be hardware-dependent and may not reflect algorithmic efficiency. The paper should include FLOPs counts for all models to allow a more principled comparison of computational cost. Additionally, the paper should report memory usage during inference, as token-mixing MLPs may have different memory footprints compared to convolutions or attention.

- Overclaiming on the necessity of large-scale pre-training: The paper states that 'when pre-trained on large datasets, Mixer attains competitive scores,' but the results on ImageNet-21k show Mixer-L/16 at 84.15% versus ViT-L/16 at 85.30%, a gap of 1.15%. The paper attributes this to overfitting but does not explore whether additional regularization or different hyperparameters could close the gap. The claim that Mixer is 'competitive' should be qualified with the observation that it underperforms ViT on medium-scale datasets, and the paper should discuss the practical implications of this limitation for researchers without access to large proprietary datasets.

- Missing FLOPs comparison for computational cost: The paper reports throughput in images/sec/core but does not provide FLOPs counts for any model. Throughput depends on hardware implementation and batch size, making it an unreliable metric for comparing algorithmic efficiency across architectures. Standard practice in this field is to report FLOPs alongside parameter counts and throughput. The paper should compute and report FLOPs for each Mixer variant and for all compared models (ViT, BiT, HaloNet) at the same input resolution. This would allow readers to assess whether Mixer's computational advantage comes from algorithmic efficiency or from implementation details.

- No controlled ablation of position embeddings: The paper claims that Mixer does not need position embeddings because token-mixing MLPs are sensitive to input order, but this claim is not tested. Section 3.4 shows Mixer is invariant to patch permutations when trained on shuffled images, but this does not demonstrate that the model learns positional information when trained on natural images. The paper should include an experiment where Mixer is trained on ImageNet with and without explicit learned position embeddings, measuring top-1 accuracy. Without this, the claim that position embeddings are unnecessary is unsupported by evidence.

- No quantitative analysis of token-mixing features: Figure 5 shows learned token-mixing weights but provides only qualitative observations about features operating on entire images versus smaller regions. The paper would benefit from systematic analysis, such as measuring the effective receptive field of token-mixing MLPs using standard techniques (e.g., gradient-based or perturbation-based methods) and comparing frequency content of learned filters to those in CNNs. Without quantitative characterization, the claim that Mixer processes information differently from CNNs remains speculative and does not advance understanding of the architecture.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add a controlled comparison of Mixer and ViT on ImageNet from scratch with matched regularization tuning, reporting the best achievable accuracy for each model.

- Include an ablation study on the effect of adding explicit position embeddings to Mixer when training on standard ImageNet.

- Provide quantitative analysis of token-mixing MLP features, such as effective receptive field measurements or frequency analysis, to support claims about learned representations.

- Report FLOPs and memory usage for all models in the main paper to enable fairer comparison of computational efficiency.

- Add a discussion section explicitly addressing the limitations of Mixer on medium-scale datasets and the conditions under which it is competitive.

comments.title: Overfitting claim lacks controlled evidence
comments.quote: from scratch on ImageNet, Mixer-B/16 achieves a reasonable top-1 accuracy of  $76.44\%$ . This is  $3\%$  behind the ViT-B/16 model. The training curves (not reported) reveal that both models achieve very similar values of the training loss. In other words, Mixer-B/16 overfits more than ViT-B/16.
comments.explanation: The claim that Mixer-B/16 overfits more than ViT-B/16 is based on unreported training curves and the observation that training losses are similar while validation accuracy differs. However, similar training losses do not necessarily imply identical model capacity or optimization dynamics; differences in validation accuracy could also arise from architectural inductive biases, regularization sensitivity, or optimization convergence. The paper does not report whether hyperparameters (e.g., weight decay, dropout, learning rate) were tuned separately for each architecture to ensure a fair comparison. Without such controls, the attribution to overfitting is speculative. The authors should either provide the training curves and a controlled ablation with matched regularization budgets, or qualify the claim as a hypothesis rather than a conclusion.

comments.title: Missing FLOPs for all models in Table 2
comments.quote:
Main results

Table 2 presents comparison of the largest Mixer models to state-of-the-art models from the literature. "ImNet" and "ReaL" columns refer to the original ImageNet validation [13] and cleaned-up ReaL [5]
comments.explanation: The table reports throughput (img/sec/core) and training cost (TPUv3 core-days) but does not report FLOPs for any model. Throughput depends on hardware implementation, batch size, and software stack, making it an unreliable metric for comparing algorithmic efficiency. Standard practice in this field is to report FLOPs alongside parameter counts and throughput. The paper should compute and report FLOPs for each Mixer variant and for all compared models (ViT, BiT, HaloNet) at the same input resolution. This would allow readers to assess whether Mixer's computational advantage comes from algorithmic efficiency or from implementation details.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about Mixer benefiting more from data than ViT
comments.quote: It appears that Mixer benefits from the growing dataset size even more than ViT. One could speculate and explain it again with the difference in inductive biases: self-attention layers in ViT lead to certain properties of the learned functions that are less compatible with the true underlying distribution than those discovered with Mixer architecture.
comments.explanation: The claim that Mixer benefits more from dataset size than ViT is not supported by the data presented. Table 3 shows that on JFT-300M with 14 epochs, ViT-L/16 achieves 85.63% top-1 accuracy while Mixer-L/16 achieves 84.82%, a gap of 0.81% in ViT's favor. On the smaller ImageNet-21k dataset, ViT-L/16 achieves 84.46% versus Mixer-L/16's 82.89%, a gap of 1.57%. This suggests that the gap actually shrinks with larger data, but the claim that Mixer benefits more is not quantitatively demonstrated. The authors should either provide a direct comparison of the rate of improvement as a function of dataset size (e.g., plotting accuracy vs. log dataset size for both architectures) or temper the speculation. The final sentence about self-attention being 'less compatible with the true underlying distribution' is speculative and not supported by any evidence in this section.

comments.title: Misleading claim about invariance to patch permutations
comments.quote: As could be expected, Mixer is invariant to the order of patches and pixels within the patches (the blue and green curves match perfectly).
comments.explanation: The claim that Mixer is 'invariant to the order of patches' is only true under the specific training condition where the same patch permutation is applied to all images during both training and evaluation. This does not demonstrate that Mixer is invariant to patch permutations in general—it only shows that the model can learn to be invariant to a fixed permutation. The experiment conflates two distinct properties: (1) the model's ability to adapt to a permuted input distribution when trained on it, and (2) the model's inherent invariance to permutations regardless of training. The latter is what the paper implies, but the experiment only tests the former. To claim invariance, the authors would need to train on natural images and then evaluate on permuted images, or train with random permutations per image. The current setup is a test of the model's capacity to learn a fixed permutation, not of its permutation invariance.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about global information exchange
comments.quote: In contrast, Mixer allows for global information exchange in the token-mixing MLPs, which begs the question whether it processes information in a similar fashion. Figure 5 shows hidden units of the first three token-mixing MLPs of Mixer trained on JFT-300M. Recall that the token-mixing MLPs allow global communication between different spatial locations. Some of the learned features operate on the entire image, while others operate on smaller regions.
comments.explanation: The claim that token-mixing MLPs 'allow global communication between different spatial locations' is stated as fact, but the visualization in Figure 5 does not demonstrate that the learned features actually perform global information exchange. The observation that 'some features operate on the entire image, while others operate on smaller regions' is purely qualitative and does not quantify the extent of spatial integration. Without a quantitative measure (e.g., effective receptive field size or a spatial mixing metric), the claim that the model achieves global mixing remains unsubstantiated. The authors should either provide a quantitative analysis of the receptive fields of token-mixing units or soften the claim to reflect that the visualization is suggestive but not conclusive.

comments.title: Missing standard deviations for Mixer models in Table 3
comments.quote: Mixer and ViT models are averaged over three fine-tuning runs, standard deviations are smaller than 0.15.
comments.explanation: The statement that standard deviations are smaller than 0.15 is vague and does not provide the actual standard deviations for each entry. Reporting a single upper bound for all entries is insufficient because it does not allow readers to assess the variability of individual results. The authors should report the actual standard deviation for each Mixer and ViT entry in Table 3, either as a separate column or as ± values in the existing cells. This is standard practice for reporting fine-tuning results and would improve the reproducibility and interpretability of the comparison.

comments.title: Claim of 0.5% advantage over BiT is contradicted by table
comments.quote: xer-H/14 achieves  $87.94\%$  top-1 accuracy on ImageNet, which is  $0.5\%$  better than BiT-ResNet152x4 and only  $0.5\%$  lower than ViT-H/
comments.explanation: The table shows BiT-R152x4 (JFT-300M) at 87.54% top-1 on ImageNet. The difference 87.94% - 87.54% = 0.40 percentage points, not 0.5%. The paper rounds 0.40 to 0.5, which is misleading. Similarly, ViT-H/14 is at 88.55%, so 88.55% - 87.94% = 0.61 percentage points, which rounds to 0.6, not 0.5. The text should report exact differences (0.40% and 0.61%) or use consistent rounding (e.g., 0.4% and 0.6%). This may seem minor, but it gives a false impression of symmetry and precision.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of token-mixing and channel-mixing roles
comments.quote: MLP-Mixer contains two types of layers: one with MLPs applied independently to image patches (i.e. “mixing” the per-location features), and one with MLPs applied across patches (i.e. “mixing” spatial information).
comments.explanation: The description of the two MLP types is ambiguous. The first type is described as 'applied independently to image patches' and 'mixing the per-location features,' which suggests it operates on features within each patch (channel-mixing). The second type is 'applied across patches' and 'mixing spatial information,' which suggests it operates across patches (token-mixing). However, the naming is reversed relative to the standard terminology used in the rest of the paper and in the literature: 'token-mixing' typically refers to mixing across patches, and 'channel-mixing' refers to mixing within a patch. The abstract should explicitly label which is which to avoid confusion. Add a clarifying sentence: 'The per-patch MLPs are called channel-mixing layers, and the cross-patch MLPs are called token-mixing layers.'

comments.title: Unqualified claim about competitive performance
comments.quote: When trained on large datasets, or with modern regularization schemes, MLP-Mixer attains competitive scores on image classification benchmarks, with pre-training and inference cost comparable to state-of-the-art models.
comments.explanation: The claim that Mixer 'attains competitive scores' is unqualified. The paper's own results show that Mixer underperforms ViT on ImageNet-1k from scratch (76.44% vs. 79.35% for ViT-B/16) and on ImageNet-21k pre-training (84.15% vs. 85.30% for ViT-L/16). 'Competitive' is vague and could mislead readers into thinking Mixer matches state-of-the-art performance across all settings. The abstract should specify the regimes where Mixer is competitive (e.g., large-scale pre-training) and acknowledge the gap in smaller data settings. Rewrite as: 'When pre-trained on large datasets (e.g., JFT-300M), MLP-Mixer attains scores competitive with state-of-the-art models, though it underperforms on smaller datasets without extensive regularization.'

comments.title: Abstract claim about MLP exclusivity is overstated
comments.quote: We present MLP-Mixer, an architecture based exclusively on multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs). MLP-Mixer contains two types of layers: one with MLPs applied independently to image patches (i.e. “mixing” the per-location features), and one with MLPs applied across patches (i.e. “mixing” spatial information).
comments.explanation: The abstract claims the architecture is 'based exclusively on multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs).' However, the Mixer architecture also includes layer normalization, skip connections, and a final classification head that typically uses a linear layer followed by softmax. While these are common in MLP-based models, they are not themselves MLPs. The claim of exclusivity is therefore technically inaccurate. Rewrite the sentence as 'We present MLP-Mixer, an architecture whose main building blocks are multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs)' to avoid overstatement.

comments.title: Inconsistent epoch counts for subset pre-training
comments.quote: To study Mixer's ability to make use of the growing number of training examples we pre-train Mixer-B/32, Mixer-L/32, and Mixer-L/16 models on random subsets of JFT-300M containing  $3\%$ ,  $10\%$ ,  $30\%$  and  $100\%$  of all the training examples for 233, 70, 23, and 7 epochs. Thus, every model is pre-trained for the same number of total steps.
comments.explanation: The claim that every model is pre-trained for the same number of total steps is mathematically inconsistent with the stated epoch counts. If the full dataset requires 7 epochs, then a 3% subset would require 7 / 0.03 ≈ 233.3 epochs to match the total number of steps, which matches the 233 epochs stated. However, a 10% subset would require 7 / 0.10 = 70 epochs (matches), a 30% subset would require 7 / 0.30 ≈ 23.3 epochs (matches 23), and the 100% subset requires 7 epochs (matches). So the arithmetic is correct, but the phrasing 'Thus, every model is pre-trained for the same number of total steps' is misleading because the total number of steps is the same only if the number of steps per epoch scales inversely with the subset fraction. The authors should clarify that the total number of gradient updates is held constant, not the total number of epochs, and that this is achieved by adjusting the number of epochs inversely with the subset size.
